topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun slanted through the dusty window of the hallway, illuminating motes dancing in the air. I was supposed to be organizing the mail, a mundane chore I usually tolerated, but today, something snagged my attention. Tucked between a utility bill and a flyer for a local bake sale was a thick, cream-colored card. It was completely blank, save for a single, elegant script at the bottom: "Happy Birthday." My stomach tightened immediately. It wasn't addressed to me. The handwriting was lovely, looping and deliberate, and it felt like a direct commentary on my own existence. I picked it up, my fingers tracing the smooth paper, a strange heat rising in my chest. Who had taken the time to craft something so thoughtful for someone else? A sharp, unpleasant knot formed just beneath my ribs. I folded it quickly, shoving it deep into my pocket, the crinkle of the paper sounding deafening in the quiet room.

[story 2]

I stared at the card for a full minute, my breath catching in my throat. It was pristine, untouched. The simple sentiment felt like an accusation. I glanced around the apartment, half-expecting to see someone else’s mail, someone who had earned such a gesture. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. It was just a card, an anonymous offering, yet it felt monumental. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to process the sudden spike of irritation. Why did it have to be someone else? Why did the attention, however small, seem to be directed elsewhere? I smoothed the card against my palm, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin, demanding an explanation that the silent paper refused to provide.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the mailbox, the sight of the card hit me like a physical blow. It was undeniably a birthday card, addressed, though the name was a stranger's. The careful calligraphy suggested an investment of time and genuine feeling. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I snatched it up, my hands trembling slightly. It was a perfect little package of acknowledgment, and the realization that this sentiment had been reserved for another person sent a wave of sharp resentment through me. I crumpled the card once, then smoothed it out again, my gaze fixed on the unwritten space where a name should have been. I wanted to know who it was for, and why they hadn't thought to send it to me. The silence of the hallway amplified the frantic drumming inside my own chest.
